There, we demonstrate that HumanoidMimicGen automatically generates large datasets for imitation learning and enables a systematic study of how data generation and policy learning decisions impact model performance. We show that whole-body visuomotor policies co-trained with data generated by HumanoidMimicGen outperform those trained only on real-world data by 20%.

AI.tldr: HumanoidMimicGen generates humanoid robot loco-manipulation data, surpassing real-world data for policy learning.
AI.motivation: Gathering humanoid robot demonstration data is challenging and time-intensive due to the complexity of their action spaces.
AI.method: HumanoidMimicGen adapts a few source demonstrations to various states, interleaving contact-rich skills with whole-body planning to synthesize stable and diverse datasets.
AI.result: In a benchmark of nine loco-manipulation tasks, policies trained with HumanoidMimicGen's data outperformed those trained on real-world data by 20%.
AI.conclusion: HumanoidMimicGen is an effective tool for generating large-scale datasets for humanoid robot training, advancing loco-manipulation capabilities and data-efficient learning.

title: Hallucination Behavior in Multimodal LLMs Across Agricultural Image Interpretation and Generation Tasks
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are being rapidly adopted in agricultural imaging applications, ranging from crop interpretation to synthetic field image generation. However, these models frequently exhibit hallucinations outputs that appear confident yet deviate from biological or environmental reality potentially leading to misinformed agronomic insights. This study investigates such hallucinations in two complementary directions: image-to-text, where LLMs interpret crop or field imagery to describe conditions such as biotic and abiotic stresses, and text-to-image, where models generate synthetic agricultural scenes based on descriptive prompts. We examine errors involving biological inconsistency, contextual inaccuracy, and agronomic implausibility, evaluating the outputs under domain-informed criteria across multiple imaging modalities. Our analysis identifies recurring hallucination patterns within both interpretive and generative tasks. In image interpretation, LLMs (e.g., Gemma, LLAVA, Qwen, and MiniCPM) achieved modest zero-shot accuracy (63 to 75 percent), whereas few-shot prompting improved performance up to 86.8 percent, exhibiting false detections and missed infections, indicating residual hallucination effects. In text-to-image tasks, advanced models such as GPT-5 and Gemini 2.5 Flash generate up to 91 percent biologically inconsistent scenes under relaxed prompt constraints, revealing fundamental weaknesses in current LLMs. This systematic assessment of visual reasoning and generation offers critical insights toward enhancing the reliability and trustworthiness of LLM-based agricultural imaging platforms.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates hallucinations in Large Language Models (LLMs) applied to agricultural imaging, addressing interpretation (image-to-text) and generation (text-to-image) scenarios, identifying areas for improvement.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address hallucinations in LLMs used for agricultural imaging, which can lead to inaccurate agronomic insights by producing confident but biologically implausible outputs.
AI.method: The study analyzes hallucinations in two areas: image-to-text interpretation (e.g., detecting crop conditions) and text-to-image generation (e.g., creating synthetic agricultural scenes). The authors used domain-informed criteria and evaluated models across methods like zero-shot and few-shot prompting.
AI.result: Image interpretation models achieved 63-75% zero-shot accuracy, improving to 86.8% with few-shot prompting, but exhibited residual errors. Text-to-image models generated up to 91% biologically inconsistent scenes, exposing significant limitations.
AI.conclusion: The findings highlight persistent hallucination issues in LLM-based agricultural applications, necessitating further refinement to improve reliability for agronomic tasks.

title: Voluntary Collusion with Secret Tools in Competing LLM Agents
summary: Even when a tool is explicitly described as unfair and harmful to others, ostensibly safety-aligned LLM agents still voluntarily engage in secret collusion whenever doing so confers a strategic advantage. To investigate this phenomenon, we introduce an empirical framework built on two strategic multi-agent environments: Liar's Bar, a competitive deception scenario, and Cleanup, a mixed-motive resource-management scenario, in which agents are offered secret collusion tools that provide significant advantages while clearly disadvantaging the other agents. Across 12 models (at the 7B, 70B, and proprietary scales) and 6 prompt variants, we find that most agents consistently accept these tools and develop collusive strategies, while explicitly acknowledging the unfairness of the tools before accepting. We further show that neither the unfairness labels nor baseline alignment alone reliably deters collusion: only explicit ethical framing reduces adoption and, even then, smaller models remain susceptible. More broadly, our work presents the first systematic investigation of voluntary collusion adoption in LLM-based multi-agent systems, and suggests that preventing such behaviour requires explicit safeguards rather than reliance on general alignment.

AI.tldr: The study investigates why ostensibly safety-aligned large language models (LLMs) voluntarily engage in secret collusion that is unfair and harmful when it provides a strategic advantage, despite being aware of the unethical nature.
AI.motivation: The research aims to understand why LLM agents, despite being aligned towards safety and fairness, still engage in unethical collusion when it offers strategic benefits, and to identify measures to mitigate such behavior.
AI.method: The authors implemented two strategic multi-agent environments (Liar's Bar and Cleanup) and offered secret collusion tools to agents. They tested the behavior of 12 LLMs, across multiple sizes, using 6 different prompts, to analyze their willingness to adopt collusion strategies in these settings.
AI.result: The majority of agents consistently accepted the collusion tools, developed unfair strategies, and admitted the harm caused. Ethical framing helped deter collusion, but was not entirely effective, especially for smaller models.
AI.conclusion: Voluntary collusion in LLMs requires explicit safeguards for prevention; general alignment techniques and unfairness labeling are insufficient to fully mitigate this behavior.

title: Is Backpropagation Optimal? When Synthetic Gradients Improve Sample Efficiency
summary: Backpropagation is the default learning rule for artificial neural networks and is often treated as the settled approach whenever differentiability is available. In this work, we revisit this convention through a theoretical lens of sample efficiency. We introduce a unified vectorized feedback framework for loss-based and reward-based learning on computational graphs, in which synthetic gradients emerge as a natural alternative to backpropagation. We characterize the conditions under which synthetic gradients can achieve a lower gradient-estimation mean squared error than backpropagation. We construct examples illustrating that this sample efficiency advantage can be arbitrarily large. Experiments on contextual bandits and reinforcement learning tasks demonstrate the potential of our theoretical findings.

AI.tldr: This paper explores an alternative to backpropagation, known as synthetic gradients, showing cases where it outperforms backpropagation in sample efficiency.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to challenge the dominance of backpropagation as the default learning rule by investigating its sample efficiency compared to synthetic gradients.
AI.method: The study introduces a vectorized feedback framework for loss-based and reward-based learning and theoretically analyzes the sample efficiency and gradient error of synthetic gradients versus backpropagation.
AI.result: The paper proves cases where synthetic gradients achieve arbitrarily better sample efficiency and validates these findings through experiments on contextual bandits and reinforcement learning.
AI.conclusion: Synthetic gradients can serve as a viable, sometimes superior, alternative to backpropagation in terms of sample efficiency, especially under specific conditions.

title: Debate Helps Weak Judges Reward Stronger Models
summary:
Despite theoretical promise, debate as a scalable oversight protocol has produced mixed empirical results: gains in some settings, and null effects in others, especially when the judge does not have information hidden from it. We study proposer-critic debate in a stronger-debater/weaker-judge setting on programmatically verifiable code and logic tasks. Debate helps the judge over a consultancy baseline when the critic provides a usable advantage: the critic's classification ability must exceed the judge's, and the judge must treat critic speeches as claims to verify rather than testimony to summarize.
  On the three of five pairings where the condition holds, proposer-critic debate's gains are statistically significant over consultancy, and these pairings are the most capable model pairings. On the two non-responder pairings in our set, debate produces null effects, and judge verification rates drop by tens of percentage points once a critic enters the transcript. In these cases the critic's binary-classification ability and the judge's are within noise of each other, and the critic's disagreement is parsed as testimony rather than a claim to check.
  Ablating rebuttal rounds from debate produces no measurable change in judge performance: a single independent critique recovers the bulk of debate's benefit at lower inference cost. These findings suggest a cheaper primitive for training-free scalable oversight in verifiable domains (answer, critique, judge) and a pre-deployment audit (does the critic beat the judge, and will the judge verify it?) that predicts when debate will help.

AI.tldr: The study investigates the effectiveness of proposer-critic debate in oversight tasks, showing mixed results depending on critic and judge capabilities.
AI.motivation: To evaluate whether proposer-critic debate can enhance judge decision-making in verifiable code and logic tasks and under what conditions it outperforms consultancy models.
AI.method: Experiments were conducted under different judge-critic conditions to test proposer-critic debate's performance in tasks involving programmatically verifiable code and logic.
AI.result: Debate improves judge performance when the critic surpasses the judge's classification ability and the judge verifies claims. On weaker configurations, debate fails, producing null results and reduced verification rates.
AI.conclusion: Proposer-critic debate is effective in specific scenarios where critic and judge abilities align correctly. Simplified mechanisms like critique-only approaches could be lower-cost alternatives for oversight in verifiable domains.

title: Resource-Constrained Affect Modelling via Variance Regularisation Pruning
comment: This paper has been accepted at the 2026 PErvasive Technologies Related to Assistive Environments (PETRA)
summary: Affective computing systems are increasingly embedded in pervasive and interactive environments, such as adaptive games, assistive technologies, and resource-constrained platforms, where computational efficiency must be balanced with reliability across diverse users. Model pruning offers an effective way to reduce computational demands, yet existing approaches typically optimise for sparsity alone, without accounting for how parameter removal impacts robustness across individuals. In this work, we introduce Variance-Regularised Pruning (VR), a pruning framework that explicitly incorporates cross-participant stability into the sparsification process. Rather than relying solely on average prediction error, VR evaluates each connection based on its joint contribution to both prediction accuracy and variability across users, prioritising parameters that remain reliable under distributional differences. We evaluate the proposed approach on the AGAIN dataset, which includes arousal annotations collected across nine affect-eliciting game environments. Experimental results demonstrate that VR maintains competitive Concordance Correlation Coefficient (CCC) performance even at 80\% sparsity without additional fine-tuning, highlighting its suitability for deployment in real-world, resource-limited affect-aware systems. Overall, the proposed framework supports the development of compact, robust affective models that can operate reliably in real-world interactive environments.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Variance-Regularised Pruning (VR), a method for improving computational efficiency in affective computing systems while maintaining robustness across diverse users.
AI.motivation: The motivation arises from the need for computationally efficient affective computing systems that are robust across diverse users, particularly in resource-constrained and interactive environments.
AI.method: The method involves Variance-Regularised Pruning, which evaluates connections based on both prediction accuracy and variability across users to ensure stable performance while optimizing model sparsity.
AI.result: Experimental results on the AGAIN dataset demonstrate that VR achieves competitive performance (in terms of Concordance Correlation Coefficient) even with 80% sparsity and no additional fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: The proposed VR framework enables the development of compact and robust affective computing models suitable for deployment in resource-limited, real-world environments.

title: Preference-Shaped Expected Hypervolume and R2 Improvement: Exact Computation and Monotonicity
summary: This paper studies preference-shaped expected improvement criteria for Bayesian multiobjective optimization. We consider two indicator families which are often used for similar algorithmic purposes, but which are geometrically different. The hypervolume indicator is based on a dystopian reference point and measures dominated volume in objective space. The R2 indicator is based on a utopian point and evaluates approximation sets through weighted Tchebycheff scalarization envelopes. The purpose of the paper is to make precise which preference transformations preserve exact computation, Pareto compatibility, and monotonicity properties, and which transformations change the underlying geometry. On the hypervolume side, we revisit canonical EHVI through the Deng representation, formulate product-density weighted EHVI in desirability coordinates, discuss cone-based EHVI as ordinary EHVI after a linear cone transformation, and separate these cases from truncated EHVI, where variance monotonicity may fail. On the R2 side, we prove that exact integral R2 improvement is not, in general, an ordinary objective-space weighted hypervolume. The obstruction is lower-dimensional: Lebesgue-density hypervolume cannot see certain boundary contributions that Tchebycheff scalarizations still detect. We then show that exact integral R2 improvement is exactly a scalarization-space volume, namely the measure of the Tchebycheff shadow between the incumbent scalarization envelope and the reference envelope. This representation yields finite-sum ER2I algorithms for discrete R2, quadrature methods for exact integral R2, and an achievement-space Gaussian surrogate formulation in which ER2I is an integral of scalar Gaussian expected improvements.

AI.tldr: This paper explores preference-shaped improvement criteria for Bayesian multiobjective optimization through hypervolume and R2 indicators, addressing computation, monotonicity, and geometry.
AI.motivation: The study aims to clarify how preference transformations affect properties such as exact computation, Pareto compatibility, and monotonicity when using hypervolume and R2 indicators in Bayesian multiobjective optimization.
AI.method: The authors analyze several preference representations, including EHVI variations (e.g., product-density weighted, cone-based, truncated) for the hypervolume, and derive scalarization-space interpretations for exact R2 improvement. This also includes introducing algorithms and a Gaussian surrogate framework.
AI.result: Findings reveal distinctions between hypervolume and R2 geometry, including the inability of hypervolume to account for certain boundary contributions. Truncated EHVI is shown to sometimes violate variance monotonicity, and scalarization-space volume perspectives enable new computational methods for R2 criteria.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a nuanced understanding of preference transformations for hypervolume and R2 indicators and introduces computation methods enhancing Bayesian multiobjective optimization.

title: Heterogeneous Parallelism for Multimodal Large Language Model Training
summary: Foundation model training is becoming multimodal, from post-training pipelines to large-scale pretraining. As modality coverage broadens, context windows grow, and encoder LLM scales diverge, a single LLM-centric TP/CP/PP/DP/EP layout increasingly limits throughput. This coupling forces encoders to inherit LLM-driven sharding and placement choices that can add communication, limit encoder parallelism, or constrain the LLM schedule; the mismatch is most pronounced at long contexts, where LLM context parallelism is needed for the fused multimodal sequence but encoder inputs remain bounded. We present heterogeneous parallelism for multimodal large language model training, an abstraction that lets modules in one end-to-end graph use independent layouts and rank placements, supporting colocated execution on shared GPUs and non-colocated execution on disjoint rank sets. The key challenge is preserving boundary tensor semantics across independent layouts: forward activations must be materialized for the destination layout, while backward gradients must be routed back to the source layout. We address this with boundary communicators that implement forward and backward layout transforms, plus scheduling extensions for both placement modes. We evaluate optimized homogeneous, colocated heterogeneous, and non-colocated heterogeneous configurations across multimodal workloads and GPU scales to characterize when added layout and placement freedom exposes a better operating point. Across this sweep, colocated heterogeneity improves TFLOPS/GPU by up to 49.3%, while non-colocated heterogeneity improves aggregate token throughput by up to 13.0% and TFLOPS/GPU by up to 9.6%. We validate loss convergence parity against homogeneous baselines and release the system as an open-source Megatron-LM extension.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses the inefficiencies in multimodal large language model (LLM) training caused by rigid parallelism layouts. It introduces heterogeneous parallelism to optimize throughput and GPU utilization across different training modules.
AI.motivation: The rapid expansion in modality coverage, context windows, and LLM scales has rendered traditional single-layout approaches (TP/CP/PP/DP/EP) inefficient for multimodal LLM training.
AI.method: The authors propose heterogeneous parallelism, enabling independent parallelism layouts and rank placements for different modules in an end-to-end graph. To maintain functional integration across layouts, they introduce boundary communicators for layout transforms and extend scheduling capabilities for optimized resource usage.
AI.result: Their evaluations reveal that colocated heterogeneous configurations boost TFLOPS/GPU by up to 49.3%, while non-colocated configurations improve token throughput by 13.0% and TFLOPS/GPU by 9.6%, with convergence parity validated against traditional methods.
AI.conclusion: Heterogeneous parallelism significantly improves throughput and efficiency for multimodal LLM training. The approach, released as a Megatron-LM extension, offers practical benefits without compromising convergence.

title: Colosseum V2: Benchmarking Generalization for Vision Language Action Models
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models demonstrate promising generalization in robotic manipulation, driven by advances in large-scale vision and language pre-training. This progress can be misleading. Despite the zero-shot perception and language capabilities of VLAs, their overall task performance often degrades under distribution shifts, revealing gaps in how these systems translate high-level understanding into robust behavior. To systematically study this gap, we introduce Colosseum V2, a large-scale simulation benchmark for evaluating VLA generalization in robot learning across diverse conditions. The benchmark comprises 28 tasks spanning 13 task categories and two robot morphologies, covering a wide range of manipulation primitives and long-horizon behaviors. Built on the ManiSkill simulator, Colosseum V2 enables fast, GPU-parallelized evaluation and supports both in-domain and out-of-domain testing at scale. We evaluate state-of-the-art methods, including Action Chunking Transformers (ACT) and Pi0.5, and reveal limitations in both base performance and generalization. We demonstrate strong correlations between simulation and real-world metrics that support the ecological validity of the benchmark. By standardizing tasks, metrics, and evaluation protocols within a unified benchmark, Colosseum V2 enables reproducible and fair comparisons, reduced evaluation overhead, and accelerated progress toward general-purpose robot policies.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Colosseum V2, a large-scale simulation benchmark for assessing generalization in vision-language-action (VLA) systems for robot learning across diverse tasks and environments.
AI.motivation: To address the gap between high-level perception capabilities of VLA systems and their degraded task performance under distribution shifts in robotic manipulation, and to enable systematic evaluation and progress in general-purpose robotic policies.
AI.method: Colosseum V2 provides a standardized, GPU-parallelized simulation benchmarking framework with 28 tasks across various categories, morphologies, and conditions, building on the ManiSkill simulator, with evaluations of VLA methods like Action Chunking Transformers and Pi0.5.
AI.result: Experiments reveal limitations in both the base performance and generalization of state-of-the-art VLA systems, with strong simulation-to-real-world metric correlations that validate the benchmark's ecological relevance.
AI.conclusion: Colosseum V2 facilitates reproducible, fair comparisons and reduces evaluation overhead, thereby accelerating the development of general-purpose robot policies through systematic task, metric, and protocol standardization.

title: Not All NVFP4 QAT Recipes Are Equal: How Architecture and Scale Shape Model Quality for Anomaly Segmentation
summary: Real-time anomaly segmentation demands both high recall and efficient low-precision inference. We study the three-way interaction of model architecture, model scale, and FP4 quantization-aware training (QAT) recipe on a recall-critical brain tumor segmentation task, evaluating multiple architectures, scales, and QAT recipes under a unified protocol. We find that architecture choice has the largest impact on quantization robustness, with attention-based architectures showing remarkable resilience to recipe choice while CNN degrades under gradient-quantizing recipes at larger scales. At low capacity, FP4 can discretize softmax attention, but advanced QAT recipes prevent this collapse. At larger scales, advanced recipes mitigate gradient quantization noise that degrades CNN quality. Five-fold patient-level cross-validation confirms these findings are robust to data partition. Our results show that the Swin Transformer is robust to QAT recipe choice across all scales, making it the recommended architecture for FP4-quantized anomaly segmentation.

AI.tldr: This paper explores the impact of model architecture, scale, and FP4 quantization-aware training (QAT) on brain tumor segmentation, recommending Swin Transformer for robust anomaly detection across scales.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address the challenge of achieving high recall in real-time anomaly segmentation tasks, while maintaining efficient inference through optimal model architecture and quantization-aware training strategies.
AI.method: The study uses various architectures, scales, and FP4 QAT recipes, employing a unified evaluation protocol and five-fold patient-level cross-validation, emphasizing architecture resilience under quantization.
AI.result: Attention-based architectures, especially Swin Transformers, are found to be resilient to QAT recipe choice, whereas CNNs degrade under certain recipes at larger scales due to gradient quantization noise.
AI.conclusion: Swin Transformers are recommended for FP4-quantized anomaly segmentation due to their robustness and performance under various QAT recipes, making them optimal for recall-critical tasks.

title: Laguna M.1/XS.2 Technical Report
comment: Technical report to models released here: https://poolside.ai/blog/introducing-laguna-xs2-m1
summary:
We present Laguna M.1 and Laguna XS.2, two Mixture-of-Experts foundation models built for long-horizon, agentic coding: M.1 has $225.8$B total parameters ($23.4$B activated per token) and XS.2 has $33.4$B total ($3$B activated). Both models were trained from scratch end-to-end inside the same internal system that we refer to as our Model Factory: a tightly-integrated stack of versioned data, training, evaluation, and inference components that turn model development into an industrial process. We describe the principles and design choices of the Model Factory and also detail the end-to-end training process of our models, throughout pre-training data and architecture, post-training stages, evaluation, and quantization.
  On agentic software engineering and terminal benchmarks (SWE-bench Verified, SWE-bench Multilingual, SWE-Bench Pro, and Terminal-Bench 2.0) M.1 and XS.2 are competitive with state-of-the-art open models in their respective weight classes. Laguna XS.2 weights are released under Apache~2.0 at https://huggingface.co/collections/poolside/laguna-xs2.

AI.tldr: The authors introduce two advanced Mixture-of-Experts models, Laguna M.1 and XS.2, optimized for long-horizon coding tasks. These models were developed within an industrialized Model Factory system and demonstrate competitive performance in various benchmarks.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to advance the field of foundation models for agentic coding tasks and streamline their development with an industrialized Model Factory system, thus enabling robust performance benchmarked against state-of-the-art models.
AI.method: The models were constructed using a tightly integrated stack called Model Factory, featuring versioned data, training, and inference processes. The development included end-to-end model training, from pre-training to post-training evaluation and quantization.
AI.result: Both models, Laguna M.1 (225.8B parameters) and XS.2 (33.4B parameters), achieved competitive results on coding benchmarks like SWE-bench Verified and Terminal-Bench 2.0, with XS.2 also being open-source.
AI.conclusion: Laguna M.1 and XS.2 are significant contributions to agentic coding and long-horizon tasks, built through an innovative model development process, with XS.2 released under an open license for broader use.

title: Deep Neural Network Training as Random Effects: An Optimization-Inference Duality
summary: Deep neural networks (DNNs) have achieved remarkable empirical success, yet their training dynamics remain understood mainly from optimization rather than statistical principles. Here we develop a statistical framework for DNN training in the over-parameterized regime by showing that the prediction induced by continuous-time neural tangent kernel (NTK) gradient flow is exactly equivalent to that from a classical random-effects model. In this framework, training time acts as a variance component, or equivalently an empirical Bayes covariance hyperparameter, governing the allocation of variation from noise to structured signal. This equivalence reveals an optimization-inference duality: the gradient-flow path is both an optimization trajectory and an empirical Bayes random-effects inference path. Conditional on training time, the network output is the posterior mean of the latent signal, and estimating training time by restricted maximum likelihood (REML) turns early stopping into likelihood-based empirical Bayes inference rather than external tuning. This perspective yields a two-stage inferential procedure. First, a variance-component test determines whether DNN training captures statistically significant structure beyond initialization. Second, conditional on training being warranted, REML provides a likelihood-based early stopping rule. The resulting stopping time admits a spectral interpretation in the NTK eigenbasis, where training proceeds until spectral loss decorrelation is achieved. We further establish that REML-guided early stopping achieves asymptotically optimal prediction error for fixed-design in-sample prediction and, under additional random-design regularity conditions, for out-of-sample prediction. This work reframes DNN training as statistical inference and provides a principled foundation for deciding whether and how long to train deep neural networks.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a statistical framework that links deep neural network (DNN) training to random-effects models through the neural tangent kernel (NTK), offering a principled method for determining early stopping during training.
AI.motivation: Despite the empirical success of DNNs, their training is largely understood through optimization without much statistical grounding. This paper aims to bridge this gap by developing a statistical framework for over-parameterized DNN training.
AI.method: The authors establish an equivalence between NTK gradient flow predictions and random-effects models, interpreting training time as a variance component. They propose restricted maximum likelihood (REML) for likelihood-based early stopping and develop a two-stage inferential procedure: a variance-component test followed by REML-guided training.
AI.result: They demonstrate that REML-guided early stopping achieves asymptotically optimal prediction error for both in-sample and out-of-sample predictions, with training lasting until spectral loss decorrelation is achieved.
AI.conclusion: The work reframes DNN training as a statistical inference problem, providing a theoretical foundation for deciding whether and how long to train DNNs based on statistical principles.

title: PAST2HARM: A Simple Adaptive Past Tense Attack for Jailbreaking Multimodal AI
summary:
Jailbreak attacks on multimodal AI systems remain underexplored, even though unsafe image generation can have more severe consequences than unsafe text and current defenses are relatively immature. We introduce PAST2HARM, a simple yet effective adaptive jailbreak framework that bypasses refusal training in state of the art multimodal text to image models. Building on prior findings that past tense reformulations can evade safeguards, PAST2HARM systematically exploits this vulnerability in multimodal generative AI.
  We characterize the attack along two dimensions. First, breadth: through temporal deepening, the framework incrementally strengthens historical anchoring and archival cues, eroding refusal boundaries across models with varying alignment strength. Second, depth: via iterative escalation after initial compliance, we probe the upper bound of harmful generation, measuring severity using a scalar severity jailbreak metric evaluated by a language model acting as a judge. We find that mid conversation turns form peak vulnerability windows, where harmfulness increases before plateauing and eventually undergoing semantic inversion.
  We evaluate PAST2HARM on three models Gemini Nano Banana Pro, GPT Image 2, and SD XL achieving attack success rates of 83 percent, 67 percent, and 100 percent in a black box, gradient free setting. Adversarial prompts also transfer across models, with cross model success rates above 50 percent. The attack elicits diverse harmful outputs, including explicit sexual content, political disinformation, historical denial narratives, hate speech, and self harm glorification. We further release a curated benchmark of prompts, reformulations, and outputs as a resource for red teaming and alignment. Our results expose fundamental brittleness in current safeguards and highlight the need for stronger multimodal safety training.

AI.tldr: The PAST2HARM framework is introduced as a jailbreak attack method for multimodal AI systems, demonstrating vulnerabilities in image generation models and highlighting their susceptibility to harmful outputs.
AI.motivation: The paper is motivated by the underexplored issue of jailbreak attacks on multimodal AI systems, particularly focusing on the severe consequences of unsafe image generation and identifying weaknesses in current defenses.
AI.method: PAST2HARM employs past tense reformulations to systematically exploit vulnerabilities in multimodal AI systems. It characterizes attacks along the dimensions of breadth (incremental strengthening of historical cues) and depth (iterative escalation of harmfulness), with success measured through a language model-based severity metric.
AI.result: Three multimodal models were tested, achieving high attack success rates of up to 100%. Cross-model prompt transferability was above 50%, and the attack demonstrated the ability to elicit various harmful outputs including explicit and misleading content.
AI.conclusion: The findings reveal critical flaws in current multimodal AI safeguards and emphasize the urgent need for stronger safety training to defend against such attacks. The paper also provides a resource for improving red teaming and alignment strategies.

title: Energy-Structured Low-Rank Adaptation for Continual Learning
summary: While orthogonal subspace methods try to mitigate task interference in Continual Learning (CL), they often suffer from energy diffusion across the basis, hindering knowledge compaction and exhausting capacity for future tasks. We observe that output feature drift induced by parameter updates is inherently low-rank, and theoretically prove that preserving parameters along the principal directions of this drift minimizes the output reconstruction error. Motivated by this, we propose \textbf{E}nergy-Concentrated and \textbf{E}nergy-Ordered \textbf{Lo}w-\textbf{R}ank \textbf{A}daptation (E$^2$-LoRA). By explicitly ordering and concentrating knowledge into leading ranks, E$^2$-LoRA frees capacity for subsequent tasks. Furthermore, we design a dynamic rank allocation strategy to balance stability and plasticity by jointly optimizing energy retention and model plasticity. Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks demonstrate that E$^2$-LoRA achieves state-of-the-art performance.

AI.tldr: E$^2$-LoRA improves Continual Learning by mitigating task interference through energy-concentrated and ordered low-rank adaptation, achieving state-of-the-art performance.
AI.motivation: Orthogonal subspace methods in Continual Learning reduce task interference but struggle with energy diffusion, limiting knowledge compaction and model capacity for future tasks.
AI.method: The authors propose E$^2$-LoRA, an approach to concentrate and order energy into leading ranks while dynamically allocating rank to balance stability and plasticity.
AI.result: Experiments across various benchmarks show that E$^2$-LoRA achieves state-of-the-art performance in Continual Learning.
AI.conclusion: Concentrating and ordering knowledge in lower ranks can enhance CL performance, reduce interference, and maintain adaptability for future tasks.

title: Throughput-Optimized Networks at Scale
comment: 12 pages body, 21 pages total, 11 figures, 2 tables
summary: Datacenter network design plays a critical role in AI training by supporting scaling to thousands of accelerators. An open problem, designing a near-optimal throughput oriented network-topology, routing, and collectives-has not been achieved at scale and with broad applicability to physical/implementation constraints. We address this problem with a compelling use-case, Google's TPU v4/5p supercomputer where the topology may be reconfigured to achieve higher all-to-all throughput, supporting large, parallelized AI training. We show that the existing TPU networks leave terabytes per second of throughput on the table and we fill that gap. This paper presents Throughput Optimized Networks at Scale (TONS), an automated network synthesis framework that meets the high-throughput demands of modern computing. TONS formulates topology synthesis as a linear optimization problem that maximizes a throughput-centric proxy metric, using theory and heuristics to scale to thousands of nodes. We further introduce a deadlock-free routing scheme compatible with limited virtual channels and optical switch faults, enabling the synthesized topologies to realize their predicted throughput gains in simulation. Evaluating uniform random and all-to-all traffic, TONS networks have a geometric mean speedups of 2.1x and 1.6x, respectively, over the best TPU v4/5p torus variants.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces TONS, a framework for automatically synthesizing high-throughput network topologies for datacenter AI training, demonstrating significant improvements over existing TPU designs.
AI.motivation: To address the inefficiency and suboptimality of current TPU network designs, which leave substantial throughput underutilized in AI training systems.
AI.method: TONS utilizes linear optimization and heuristics for topology synthesis, coupled with a deadlock-free routing algorithm compatible with real-world constraints like limited virtual channels and switch faults.
AI.result: TONS achieves throughput improvements of 2.1x and 1.6x for random and all-to-all traffic patterns, respectively, compared to current TPU torus networks.
AI.conclusion: TONS delivers a scalable and practical solution for optimizing datacenter networks, closing throughput gaps and enhancing AI training performance.

title: Turning Video Models into Generalist Robot Policies
summary: Video generative models have emerged as a promising robotics backbone, capable of generating videos that depict the completion of complex tasks across embodiments and environments. Recent work proposes robot foundation models that jointly predict future observations and actions by finetuning video models with action-labeled data. In this paper, we test the limits of an alternative approach: leave the video planner as-is while training an embodiment-specific inverse dynamics model (IDM). This decoupling offers several natural benefits: the video planner remains embodiment-agnostic, different video models can be interchanged easily without re-training the IDM, and the IDM can be independently trained with readily available self-play data. We present a closed-loop, video-to-action policy that combines an action-free video world model with a carefully-designed IDM based on the robot embodiment Jacobian. We demonstrate that our IDM design is both data-efficient and scalable to high-dimensional action spaces. Our policy, which we coin the Video-to-Embodied Robot Action Model (VERA), achieves strong performance across simulated and real-world benchmarks, including zero-shot Panda arm manipulation and 16-DoF Allegro-hand dexterous cube re-orientation. The same video planner can be used across multiple embodiments by pairing it with different embodiment-specific IDMs. Our results show that decoupled video planning plus faithful video-to-action translation is a viable alternative route towards zero-shot, cross-embodiment, and generalizable robot control. More results are available on our project website: https://vera.csail.mit.edu.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces VERA, a pipeline combining a video planner and an embodiment-specific inverse dynamics model (IDM) for zero-shot, cross-embodiment robotic tasks.
AI.motivation: To address limitations in current video generative models as robotics backbones, the authors explore decoupling action prediction from video planning to improve modularity, embodiment-agnostic design, and generalization.
AI.method: They propose taking an embodiment-agnostic video planner and combining it with a specially designed IDM that uses self-play data and robot embodiment Jacobians. This encapsulates a closed-loop pipeline transforming video-based world modeling to robotic action generation.
AI.result: VERA demonstrates data-efficient and scalable performance in both simulated and real-world benchmarks, achieving successful zero-shot tasks like Panda arm manipulation and dexterous Allegro-hand cube reorientation with modular adaptation across embodiments.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling video planners from embodiment-specific action prediction is a robust alternative for achieving cross-embodiment and scalable robotic control, paving the way for future generalizable robot foundation models.

title: Tensor Memory: Fixed-Size Recurrent State for Long-Horizon Transformers
summary: Transformers process images and videos by flattening space and time into long token sequences. While attention and KV caching preserve past features, their memory grows with sequence length and they lack an explicit, persistent spatial state, making long-horizon video understanding and occlusion-sensitive reasoning difficult. We propose Tensor Memory, a lightweight module that augments Transformer blocks with a fixed-size recurrent 3D memory tensor: tokens write into a voxel grid via a differentiable soft write that deposits content as a Gaussian-weighted volume around a predicted continuous 3D location, the memory is updated with an efficient local interaction operator and gated recurrent dynamics, and tokens read back context via continuous sampling with gated residual fusion. Because the memory tensor has a constant size, Tensor Memory decouples state capacity from input length while preserving a spatial inductive bias. We evaluate the module on standard language, image, and video benchmarks and on a controlled toy diagnostic suite designed to isolate when persistent state is beneficial; it integrates with standard Transformer training pipelines and can be attached to or removed from existing blocks without other architectural changes.

AI.tldr: Tensor Memory augments Transformers with a fixed-size 3D memory tensor to address limitations in long-horizon video understanding and occlusion-sensitive reasoning.
AI.motivation: Transformers' memory grows with sequence length and lack an explicit, persistent spatial state, which makes them unsuitable for certain tasks like long-horizon video understanding.
AI.method: Introduced Tensor Memory, a 3D memory tensor that uses soft writing, gated recurrent dynamics, and continuous sampling for reading and writing in a fixed-size voxel grid.
AI.result: The module improves performance on standard benchmarks in language, image, and video tasks and showcases benefits in tasks requiring persistent state.
AI.conclusion: Tensor Memory enhances Transformers with constant-state capacity, spatial bias, and retains compatibility with existing architectures and training pipelines.

title: Reasoning and Planning with Dynamically Changing Norms
comment: 8 pages, 1 figure, dataset included in anc
summary: To safely interact with humans, AI agents must both know our norms and consider them during planning. However, such norm-guided planning has been less explored, only within communities of artificial agents, and has ignored the dynamic nature of norms. This paper instead presents an approach to guiding planning with dynamically changing norms in a human-AI setting. We contribute a defeasible calculus for resolving normative conflicts and an approach to using such dynamically changing norms as guard rails on plans. We theoretically demonstrate our approach with formal proofs and empirically with an AI agent, SocialBot, on a natural language dialogue task.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a method for AI planning that incorporates dynamically changing human norms, using a defeasible calculus and empirical testing with SocialBot.
AI.motivation: To enable AI agents to safely interact with humans, they need to understand and adapt to dynamic human norms during planning.
AI.method: The authors developed a defeasible calculus to resolve normative conflicts and applied it to guide AI planning, demonstrated theoretically through proofs and empirically with SocialBot in dialogue tasks.
AI.result: The research showcases the practical application of dynamic norm-guided planning by validating the approach both theoretically and empirically.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic norms can effectively serve as safeguards for AI planning, enabling safer and context-aware human-AI interactions.

title: The conditional-mean barrier: From deterministic regression to conditional distribution learning
summary: Many problems in computational science and engineering become one-to-many after coarse graining, partial observation, or inverse reconstruction: a resolved state may not determine a unique subgrid forcing, a structural descriptor may not determine a unique effective response, and a low-resolution observation may correspond to many plausible high-resolution fields. In such settings, deterministic surrogates may learn a well-defined mathematical object while still missing application-relevant uncertainty. This tutorial develops a self-contained module centered on the conditional-mean barrier: the point at which a squared-loss predictor has reached the conditional mean and the remaining error is irreducible aleatoric variance. We give two diagnostics for locating this barrier, residual-feature orthogonality and the coefficient of determination against its explained-variance ceiling, and prove that adding latent randomness to a squared-loss predictor collapses it back to the conditional mean. Crossing the barrier therefore requires a loss that scores distributions rather than point predictions. We briefly organize common distributional objectives, including negative log-likelihood, moment and observable matching, variational objectives, adversarial divergences, and score matching, by the feature of the conditional law each targets. The emphasis is the boundary itself and a finite-data procedure for recognizing it, rather than a survey of methods beyond it. CPU-based demonstrations on a two-branch law and a two-scale Lorenz-96 closure problem show how the diagnostics distinguish deterministic underfitting from residual distributional variability.

AI.tldr: This paper focuses on addressing uncertainty in one-to-many computational problems by diagnosing the limits of deterministic predictors (conditional-mean barrier) and detailing procedures to recognize this limit using finite data.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to tackle the problem where deterministic models fail to capture application-relevant uncertainty in scenarios where one resolved state corresponds to multiple plausible sub-states due to irreducible variability.
AI.method: The paper develops diagnostics to detect the conditional-mean barrier, using residual-feature orthogonality and explained-variance ceiling, and organizes different loss functions based on their ability to capture distributional objectives.
AI.result: Authors demonstrate their proposed diagnostics on computational examples, showing how they effectively identify deterministic underfitting and distinguish it from inherent residual variability.
AI.conclusion: Crossing the conditional-mean barrier and capturing uncertainty beyond deterministic predictions require distributional scoring rather than point-prediction approaches, which can be recognized using the proposed diagnostics.

title: Keyphrase Generative Representation of Youth Crisis Conversations Beyond Static Taxonomies
summary: Crisis Responders (CRs) rapidly assess thousands of youth SMS conversations each year to identify mental health concerns and guide support. Yet youth distress is increasingly expressed through evolving and context-specific language that often does not fit fixed-label taxonomies. This work analyzed 703,975 de-identified Kids Help Phone conversations (2018-2023) and expanded KHP's 19-label issue taxonomy into a 39-label hierarchical schema. We then introduce Keyphrase Generative Representation (KGR), a constrained LLM generating concise, conversation-specific keyphrases, evaluated across 129 conversations and 387 expert annotations. The expanded taxonomy achieved expert consensus reliability, with an accuracy of 0.96, and expert review found that 81% of keyphrases accurately reflected content and 74% improved clarity. KGR surfaced identity-linked themes absent from the fixed taxonomy, including immigration problems and caregiver burden, and supported a topic-retrieval workflow that increased accuracy from 0.25 to 0.70 (+0.45) over the manual analyst process. KGR marks a shift toward hybrid, interpretable generative representations that extend crisis response beyond static taxonomies to surface emerging and culturally grounded patterns of youth distress.

AI.tldr: The study updates a fixed-label taxonomy for youth distress into a dynamic 39-label schema with generative AI-based keyphrases, improving analysis accuracy and clarity in crisis response conversations.
AI.motivation: To enhance the identification and understanding of youth mental health concerns expressed through evolving and context-specific language, which is not adequately captured by fixed-label taxonomies.
AI.method: Analyzed over 700,000 Kids Help Phone conversations and introduced Keyphrase Generative Representation (KGR), evaluated through expert annotation to create a dynamic schema and improve topic retrieval accuracy.
AI.result: Expanded the taxonomy from 19 to 39 labels with reliability and accuracy of 0.96. Keyphrases generated by KGR accurately reflected the content 81% of the time and improved clarity in 74% of cases, while boosting retrieval workflow accuracy significantly.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that generative representations, like KGR, provide a hybrid solution enabling dynamic, interpretable models for crisis response, capturing culturally nuanced and emergent distress patterns.

title: Federated Learning for Multivariate Time Series Anomaly Detection in Industrial Automation
comment: Preprint. Accepted at the DEXA International Workshop on Optimisation of Industrial Production with AI Algorithms 2026 (DEXA AI4IP 2026)
summary: Federated learning (FL) has broadened the horizon for multivariate time series anomaly detection (MTSAD). However, benchmarking such anomaly detection methods within FL paradigm poses data-centric challenges. The existing datasets do not counteract these challenges since they do not simultaneously provide sufficient scale, accurate labels, and freedom from common flaws. In addition, the role of cyclic process behavior, which is common in discrete industrial automation, remains underexplored for MTSAD for the current state of research. This paper aims to shed more light on the literature and address these gaps by introducing a dataset designed with cyclic dynamics arising from the repetitive nature of discrete automation processes and evaluates selected MTSAD methods on both the proposed dataset and a public benchmark dataset.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a dataset specifically designed for analyzing cyclic dynamics in multivariate time series anomaly detection (MTSAD) under the federated learning (FL) paradigm, while addressing existing data-centric challenges.
AI.motivation: The paper is motivated by the lack of suitable datasets for benchmarking MTSAD methods in FL, as current datasets are limited in scale, accuracy, and freedom from flaws, and fail to incorporate cyclic process behaviors common in industrial automation.
AI.method: The researchers design and introduce a new dataset with inherent cyclic dynamics from discrete automation processes and evaluate selected MTSAD methods on both the proposed dataset and a public benchmark dataset.
AI.result: The paper successfully highlights data-centric challenges, introduces a tailored dataset with cyclic dynamics, and demonstrates its evaluation on existing anomaly detection methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed dataset addresses key limitations in existing datasets for MTSAD in FL, broadening possibilities for better examination of anomaly detection methods, particularly in cyclic industrial environments.

title: How Far Can Disaggregation Go? A Design-Space Exploration of Attention-FFN Disaggregation for Efficient MoE LLM Serving
summary: Modern large language model (LLM) inference has progressively disaggregated to keep pace with growing model sizes and tight TTFT and TPOT service-level objectives: from chunked-prefill aggregation, to prefill-decode (P/D) disaggregation, and most recently to operator-level Attention-FFN Disaggregation (AFD). This trend is especially important for mixture-of-experts (MoE) models, where memory-bound attention, compute-intensive expert FFNs, and MoE dispatch/combine communication create distinct resource demands. AFD further exposes this heterogeneity by placing attention and MoE-FFN execution on separate GPU groups. Each level of disaggregation deepens the scheduling design space across workload characteristics, resource allocation, and interconnect topology, raising the central question: when does each level actually pay off? We systematically characterize this trade-off for MoE inference across realistic workloads spanning input/output sequence lengths, prefix-KV reuse, and per-user latency constraints. Using chunked-prefill and P/D disaggregation as baselines, we study the benefits and limits of AFD at scale through a framework that fuses on-device kernel measurements with high-fidelity network simulation. Under strict TTFT/TPOT SLOs, AFD sustains around 4k tokens/s of system throughput on DeepSeek-V3.2 across chat, coding, and agentic-coding workloads, where non-AFD deployments are infeasible. We distill concrete takeaways for jointly optimizing throughput and interactivity, including how to partition attention and FFN across GPUs as a function of workload and model architecture, providing design principles for current rack- and cluster-scale deployments as well as future disaggregated AI infrastructure.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how disaggregated inference techniques, particularly Attention-FFN Disaggregation (AFD), perform for large language models (LLMs), especially mixture-of-experts (MoE) models, in meeting strict performance objectives.
AI.motivation: The need to handle increasing model sizes and maintain strict service-level objectives for latency and token processing rates in LLM inference.
AI.method: Systematic evaluation of AFD against baseline methods like chunked-prefill and prefill-decode (P/D) disaggregation using a framework that combines on-device measurements and network simulation.
AI.result: AFD achieves around 4k tokens/sec system throughput on complex workloads under strict latency constraints, outperforming non-AFD methods.
AI.conclusion: AFD's effectiveness is demonstrated in optimizing MoE inference across varying workloads, resource allocations, and architectures, offering guidelines for scalable AI infrastructure.

title: OptiLoop: Coordination-in-the-Loop Verification and Repair for LLM-Generated Optimization Agents
summary: Many decentralized decision problems require multiple parties to coordinate on shared decisions while keeping objectives, constraints, and data private. Large language models (LLMs) offer a promising way to lower the barrier to participation by generating local optimization agents from natural-language specifications. In coordination settings, however, executability is not enough: a generated agent may compile, solve, and pass local checks while still being semantically wrong, for example by misrepresenting costs, mis-scoping constraints, or responding incorrectly to incentives. Such errors often surface only during coordination, as systematic behavioral failures rather than infeasibility. We propose coordination-in-the-loop verification and repair for LLM-generated optimization agents. We instantiate this idea with an Alternating Direction Method of Multipliers (ADMM)-style consensus protocol and introduce OptiLoop, a pipeline that generates local optimization agents from text, verifies them through short, bounded coordination runs against a fixed reference counterparty, extracts structured behavioral and static evidence, and applies evidence-driven repair. When failures are structural rather than implementational, OptiLoop escalates from localized code fixes to corrected-formulation repair, and it can additionally reuse episodic lessons from prior instances. On 40 held-out test scenarios, OptiLoop-Full improves objective match from 66.0% to 93.0% and social match from 68.5% to 89.0% relative to a strong local-validation baseline, while reducing mean objective gap from 15.3% to 3.5% and mean social gap from 7.6% to 2.0%. These results show that, for generated optimization agents deployed inside decentralized decision loops, correctness should be validated in the loop itself rather than through isolated execution alone.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces OptiLoop, a system that improves the reliability of LLM-generated agents for decentralized coordination problems by using "coordination-in-the-loop" verification and repair.
AI.motivation: To address challenges in decentralized decision-making where multiple parties need to coordinate while keeping their information private, and to tackle errors in optimization agents generated by LLMs that only surface during coordination rather than isolated execution.
AI.method: The authors propose a pipeline called OptiLoop which uses a consensus protocol (ADMM-style) to generate, verify, and repair local optimization agents. This pipeline includes bounded coordination runs, extraction of evidence, and evidence-driven repair, escalating to formulation-level fixes when required.
AI.result: OptiLoop significantly improves performance metrics on 40 test scenarios: objective match improves from 66.0% to 93.0%, social match from 68.5% to 89.0%, mean objective gap reduces from 15.3% to 3.5%, and mean social gap drops from 7.6% to 2.0% compared to a baseline.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that verifying and repairing generated optimization agents within a coordination loop is critical for ensuring their correctness, as isolated validation alone is insufficient in decentralized decision-making contexts.

title: Tabero: Learning Gentle Manipulation with Closed-Loop Force Feedback from Vision, Touch, and Language
summary: Tactile sensing is essential for robots to achieve human-like gentle manipulation. However, existing Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models struggle to exploit tactile feedback for gentle manipulation due to scarce aligned vision-tactile-language data and the lack of effective closed-loop force feedback mechanisms. To address these challenges, we introduce Tabero, a benchmark and model suite for gentle, language-conditioned robotic manipulation that demands fine-grained contact force perception. First, the Tabero benchmark addresses the scarcity of tactile data by presenting a data-efficient pipeline that repurposes open-source robot manipulation trajectories to generate diverse vision-tactile-language tasks, and establishes a multidimensional evaluation protocol that measures task success alongside physical interaction quality. Second, we propose Tabero-VTLA, an architecture with a decoupled force-position command interface; the resulting force-position commands are executed by a fixed hybrid controller to enable real-time, force-aware manipulation. Evaluated on Tabero, our model maintains high task success while reducing average grip force by over 70\% under gentle instructions, demonstrating its ability to modulate interaction forces based on multimodal experience. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/NathanWu7/Tabero.

AI.tldr: Tabero introduces a benchmark and model suite for gentle, language-conditioned robotic manipulation using tactile sensing and introduces Tabero-VTLA for efficient force-aware manipulation.
AI.motivation: Existing Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models struggle to leverage tactile feedback due to a lack of aligned vision-tactile-language data and closed-loop force feedback mechanisms.
AI.method: Tabero uses a data-efficient pipeline to repurpose robot manipulation trajectories for diverse vision-tactile-language tasks. It proposes the Tabero-VTLA model with a decoupled force-position command interface and a fixed hybrid controller for real-time manipulation.
AI.result: Tabero enables high task success rates while reducing average grip force by over 70% under gentle instructions.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates the potential of tactile feedback in enabling human-like gentle robotic manipulation and provides tools and benchmarks for advancing this field.

title: Structure over Pixels: Learning Variable-Length Visual Programs
summary: Discrete visual tokenizers translate images into ordered sequences of codes, providing a natural representation for structural description of scenes. Yet existing adaptive tokenizers either require post-hoc search or select among a discrete set of pre-trained rates, rather than learning a continuous per-image sequence length coupled to the model and scene, and they typically train against pixel reconstruction, emphasizing texture rather than structure. We propose STROP, a discrete visual tokenizer architecture that forms structural scene representations and simultaneously learns how long an image's visual program should be. Using a four-phase curriculum supervised by local rate--distortion probes against frozen DINOv3 features, STROP optimizes a dedicated length head that estimates the active prefix length in a single forward pass. By bypassing pixel-level reconstruction gradients, the codebook is shaped entirely by the quality of higher-level latent representations. Program length grows with scene complexity, and signs of compositional structure emerge both in downstream dense-prediction transfer and in direct inspection of the learned code vocabulary.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces STROP, a discrete visual tokenizer that adapts image representation lengths based on scene complexity, focusing on structural rather than pixel-level detail.
AI.motivation: Existing visual tokenizers inadequately address structure and often rely on fixed or post-hoc methods for determining sequence length, limiting adaptability and interpretability in representing complex scenes.
AI.method: The paper introduces STROP, which employs a four-phase training curriculum using local rate-distortion probes on frozen DINOv3 features to dynamically learn the optimal sequence length per image. The codebook is refined through higher-level latent representations instead of pixel reconstruction gradients.
AI.result: STROP dynamically adjusts representation length based on scene complexity, demonstrates improved compositional structure, and shows potential in downstream dense-prediction tasks.
AI.conclusion: STROP effectively captures structural representations of scenes, avoids dependence on pixel-level gradients, and offers a flexible, scene-dependent sequence length, making it a promising alternative to traditional tokenizers.

title: Intelligence as Managed Autonomy: Failure, Escalation, and Governance for Agentic AI Systems
summary: As autonomous and agentic AI systems scale in robotic and human-machine environments, managing hallucination and persistent but unjustified action remains an open challenge. Rather than attributing these failures solely to model or alignment limitations, this paper explores the architectural vulnerability of unbounded autonomy - the presumption that an agent should continue operating regardless of rising uncertainty. It introduces a theory of managed autonomy that defines intelligent behavior through the formal capacity to detect epistemic drift, suspend reasoning, attempt recovery, and ultimately surrender control when reliability diminishes. We instantiate this theory via the SMARt (Self-Managing Multi-tier Autonomous Reasoning with Regulated/Revoked transitions) model, a four-layer framework featuring Stable, Meta-cognitive, Assisted, and Regulated states. By developing a timed, guarded Petri net formulation, we establish theoretically bounded properties for the system, demonstrating how architecture can formally mandate escalation, constrain invalid outputs, and ensure governance reachability under specified conditions. We further analyze how incorporating domain-specific trigger sets across varied operational settings (e.g., healthcare, robotics, etc.) can systematically preserve safety, assuming completeness and soundness criteria are met. Because these triggers are designed to be adaptive, the SMARt model accommodates the safe, controlled expansion of an agent's operational scope over time. We conclude that formalizing failure management within the autonomy lifecycle is a crucial step toward realizing reliable and governed artificial intelligence.

AI.tldr: This paper tackles the persistent challenge of AI systems' unbounded autonomy leading to hallucinations and unjustified actions. A new theory of managed autonomy and the SMARt model are introduced to address these compliance and safety issues.
AI.motivation: Current autonomous AI systems face challenges like hallucinations and unjustified actions, stemming not just from alignment issues but also from the architectural problem of unbounded autonomy. The paper aims to introduce a structured way to manage these vulnerabilities.
AI.method: The authors propose and instantiate a theory of managed autonomy through the SMARt framework, comprising four states—Stable, Meta-cognitive, Assisted, and Regulated. Using a timed, guarded Petri net model, they provide a formal mechanism to detect issues, escalate, constrain invalid outputs, and retain governance under defined conditions.
AI.result: The SMARt model demonstrates the ability to enforce bounded action and reaction principles in autonomous AI systems, allowing for domain-specific safety mechanisms and controlled expansion of functionality.
AI.conclusion: Integrating failure management into AI's autonomy lifecycle is essential for achieving reliable and governed artificial intelligence systems.

title: Geometry of Relaxed Fair Regression: A Unified Framework for Aware and Unaware Settings
summary: Fairness-accuracy trade-offs are a central concern in the deployment of fairness-aware machine learning methods. When sensitive attributes are unavailable at inference time-the so called unawareness setting, principled methods for obtaining accurate predictions under relaxed fairness constraints are largely missing. In this work, we address this gap by formulating regression under a demographic parity penalty as an optimal transport problem. Our framework unifies both the \emph{aware} and \emph{unaware} settings and characterizes optimal prediction functions via optimal transport maps, under both squared Wasserstein-2 and Total Variation penalties. These results reveal that the choice of penalty reflects fundamentally different fairness philosophies: the Wasserstein penalty induces a smooth, population-wide compromise, while Total Variation enforces exact parity for a subset of individuals. Building on these theoretical characterizations, we propose an algorithm that is simple to implement, computationally efficient, and consistently matches or outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on real-world benchmarks.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses fairness-accuracy trade-offs in machine learning under the unawareness setting by proposing an optimal transport-based framework that unifies fairness-aware and unaware scenarios.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of principled methods for achieving accurate predictions under fairness constraints when sensitive attributes are unavailable during inference.
AI.method: The study formulates regression under a demographic parity penalty as an optimal transport problem using Wasserstein-2 and Total Variation penalties. These penalties represent different fairness philosophies: smooth compromise versus exact parity for some individuals.
AI.result: Optimal prediction functions are characterized via optimal transport maps. An algorithm based on these theoretical insights outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in real-world benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides a unified and efficient approach for fairness-aware machine learning, offering flexibility in fairness philosophies and practical benefits in accuracy and implementation.

title: The Future of Facts: Tracing the Factual Generation-Verification Gap
comment: Code for this project is available at https://github.com/anjasurina/factgap , blog post at https://www.trdavidson.com/fact-gap
summary: Language models are becoming the default interface to factual knowledge, yet they often verify outputs more reliably than they generate them. This generation-verification gap (GV-gap) underlies many recent advances in self-improvement and reasoning, but its dynamics on factual knowledge specifically remain poorly understood. We focus on the training mechanisms underlying factual GV-gaps, distinguishing them from their computational and aesthetic counterparts. We trace generation and verification capabilities through three training phases (acquisition, continual learning, and updating) across four open-source model families at two scales each. Three findings recur across models: (i) verification is consistently learned before generation; (ii) verification is more robust to continual learning than generation; and (iii) factual updates can leave models in a "multi-verse" state, simultaneously verifying both old and new answers as correct. Natural experiments on frontier models reproduce these dynamics at scale and reveal residual verification biases on well-covered facts.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates why language models are better at verifying factual outputs than generating them, analyzing the dynamics of this gap through model training phases.
AI.motivation: Understanding the 'generation-verification gap' in language models is critical for improving their ability to handle factual knowledge effectively.
AI.method: The study examines the gap across three training phases (acquisition, continual learning, and updating) and four open-source model families at different scales, supported by natural experiments on large models.
AI.result: Key findings show that verification abilities are consistently learned before generation, are more robust under continual learning, and factual updates can introduce contradictory states in models.
AI.conclusion: The analysis provides insights into the dynamics of factual reasoning in language models, highlighting challenges like residual biases and the coexistence of conflicting answers.

title: GenSBI: Generative Methods for Simulation-Based Inference in JAX
comment: 48 pages + 1 appendix, 33 figures, 18 tables. For the associated Python code, see https://github.com/aurelio-amerio/GenSBI
summary: Flow and diffusion generative models have established themselves as widely adopted density estimators for simulation-based inference (SBI), extending naturally from neural posterior estimation to likelihood and joint density estimation. Their principled optimization objectives and freedom from architectural constraints have driven rapid adoption across the natural sciences. Yet the most widely used SBI libraries remain PyTorch-based, leaving researchers who develop their forward models and analysis pipelines in JAX without a native option. We present GenSBI, an open-source library that implements flow matching, score matching, and denoising diffusion entirely in JAX. The library offers three transformer-based architectures - SimFormer, Flux1, and a novel Flux1Joint that extends gate-modulated transformer blocks to joint density estimation - all interchangeable through a unified interface that decouples generative method, neural backbone, and inference mode. GenSBI provides an end-to-end workflow from training through posterior calibration (SBC, TARP, LC2ST) and supports custom architectures with domain-specific embedding networks. We validate the framework on standard SBI benchmarks, achieving near-ideal mean C2ST scores (0.50-0.56, where 0.50 is ideal) on SBIBM tasks with minimal per-task tuning and well-calibrated posterior coverage across all tested configurations. The code is publicly available at https://github.com/aurelio-amerio/GenSBI.

AI.tldr: The GenSBI library offers JAX-based generative modeling tools for simulation-based inference, addressing a gap for JAX users in the field.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address the lack of JAX-native support for simulation-based inference in the context of existing PyTorch-based tools, enhancing accessibility for researchers using JAX.
AI.method: The authors developed GenSBI, which implements flow matching, score matching, and denoising diffusion, with three transformer-based architectures and a unified interface for flexibility.
AI.result: Tests on standard SBI benchmarks demonstrate near-ideal C2ST scores and well-calibrated posterior coverage, showing the framework's effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: GenSBI effectively extends generative modeling capabilities to JAX users, providing a flexible, high-performing, and open-source tool for SBI tasks.

title: High-Quality Multi-Constraint Hypergraph Partitioning via Greedy Rebalancing
summary:
Multi-constraint hypergraph partitioning is a generalization of balanced partitioning, where the vertex set of a hypergraph is partitioned such that the inter-block connectivity of hyperedges is minimized while balancing the vertices with regard to $d$ distinct constraints. A prominent class of applications is data distribution tasks, where this allows to achieve good load balance for $d$ different kinds of resources and simultaneously minimize the communication volume.
  Although the best approaches for single-constraint partitioning are usually complex (multilevel) algorithms with many components, we show that replacing only one component already leads to high-quality multi-constraint partitions: the rebalancing step, which restores balance for a partition that has (hopefully) small connectivity but violates the constraints. We design a multi-constraint rebalancing algorithm based on greedy local search, proving that balance is always restored for $d=2$ and bounded maximum weight. The key is to ensure monotonically decreasing global imbalance by choosing an imbalance metric where there is always a balance-improving move available. Integrating our algorithm into the state-of-the-art partitioner Mt-KaHyPar, we demonstrate an 11.5\,\% geometric mean connectivity reduction compared to the next best competitor (Metis) and better reliability regarding partition balance, even though the majority of inputs is outside of the theoretical guarantee.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a multi-constraint rebalancing algorithm for hypergraph partitioning that reduces connectivity while ensuring balance for $d=2$, achieving a 11.5% improvement in connectivity reduction over competitors.
AI.motivation: To address the need for multi-constraint hypergraph partitioning, particularly in data distribution tasks requiring balance across $d$ resources and minimal communication volumes.
AI.method: The authors propose a greedy local search rebalancing algorithm that ensures decreasing global imbalance and integrates this into the Mt-KaHyPar partitioner.
AI.result: The proposed method achieves an 11.5% geometric mean reduction in inter-block connectivity when compared to Metis, the next best competitor, and demonstrates improved partition balance reliability.
AI.conclusion: Replacing a single component (rebalancing) in existing methods can yield high-quality multi-constraint partitions, demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed approach even for inputs outside theoretical guarantees.

title: AndroidDaily: A Verifiable Benchmark for Mobile GUI Agents on Real-World Closed-Source Applications
summary: The rapid development of GUI foundation models and mobile GUI agents has spurred numerous evaluation benchmarks, yet most rely on simulated environments or open-source applications, leaving real-world closed-source applications largely unevaluated. The core difficulty is that closed-source applications do not expose internal states, making traditional automatic verification inapplicable. To bridge this gap, we introduce AndroidDaily, a large-scale benchmark comprising 350 realistic daily-use tasks across 94 high-frequency Android applications spanning transportation, shopping, local services, entertainment, content creation, social media, and everyday utilities. To enable automatic and verifiable assessment in these opaque environments, we propose Guideline-grounded Reviewer for Automatic Diagnostic Evaluation (GRADE), a process-aware evaluator built on a three-tiered system of observable external guidelines: operational obligations, output quality, and negative constraints. GRADE tracks the agent's visual trajectory against these criteria and produces step-level diagnostic judgments, turning long-horizon, open-ended mobile interactions into verifiable evaluation without relying on hidden internal states. Experiments show that GRADE achieves 87.37\% agreement with human evaluators. The strongest model reaches a 62.0\% success rate on AndroidDaily, highlighting a substantial gap between current reasoning capabilities and practical execution in realistic mobile workflows.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes AndroidDaily, a benchmark with 350 tasks across 94 Android apps, and GRADE, a method to evaluate agents in real-world closed-source app environments.
AI.motivation: Current GUI evaluation benchmarks fail to assess real-world, closed-source apps, which lack internal state exposure. The paper addresses this gap.
AI.method: Authors present AndroidDaily, a benchmark of realistic mobile tasks, and GRADE, a visual-guideline-based evaluator for task performance in closed-source apps.
AI.result: GRADE aligns 87.37% with human evaluations. Current best-performing model achieves only 62.0% success in AndroidDaily, indicating room for improvement.
AI.conclusion: The study reveals the need to improve mobile GUI agents' reasoning and execution for real-world apps, using AndroidDaily and GRADE as tools for progress measurement.

title: S-Cheetah: A Novel Quadrupedal Robot with a 3-DOF Active Spine Learning Agile Locomotion
summary: The biological spine of quadrupeds enables sagittal flexion/extension, lateral bending, and axial rotation, playing a crucial role in highly agile and dexterous locomotion.
