AI.method: The method involves using web search snippets as a first-pass evidence layer for extracting firm relationships using large language models (LLMs), followed by data evaluation for efficiency and coverage.
AI.result: The snippet-based approach is 251.2x more efficient than exhaustive full-text analysis and uncovers 7.2x more firms and 9.3x more relationships compared to traditional disclosure-based databases.
AI.conclusion: The proposed snippet-driven SCKG method is an efficient and scalable tool for improving the breadth and depth of supply-chain knowledge, with audit-friendly metadata as an added benefit.

title: Latent-Conditioned Parameterized Quantum Circuits as Universal Approximators for Distributions over Quantum States
summary: Many applications in quantum simulation, quantum chemistry, and quantum machine learning require not a single quantum state but an ensemble of states characterizing the heterogeneity of a target system. Preparing such ensembles state-by-state is prohibitive in both variational and fault-tolerant settings, motivating a generative-modeling approach. We introduce latent-conditioned parameterized quantum circuits (LPQCs), a hybrid quantum-classical framework in which classical neural networks map a latent variable sampled from a prior distribution to the parameters of a parameterized quantum circuit. We prove that LPQCs are universal approximators for probability measures over density operators in the $1$-Wasserstein distance, extending classical universal approximation theorems to the quantum-distribution setting. We additionally introduce a multimodal latent prior and a mixture-of-experts circuit architecture, and show that it empirically alleviates the barren plateau problem during optimization. Numerical experiments validate the framework on a synthetic multi-cluster ensemble of mixed quantum states and on a QM9-derived ensemble of 3-D molecular structures. In these tasks, LPQC outperforms recent quantum generative baselines while remaining competitive with typical classical baselines at substantially reduced output dimensionality. By leveraging classical expressivity in the latent space, LPQCs offer a tractable route to quantum generative modeling.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Latent-Conditioned Parameterized Quantum Circuits (LPQCs), a hybrid quantum-classical generative framework for preparing ensembles of quantum states efficiently.
AI.motivation: The motivation arises from the prohibitive cost of preparing ensembles of quantum states state-by-state, necessary for quantum simulation, quantum chemistry, and quantum machine learning.
AI.method: The authors propose LPQCs, where classical neural networks sample a latent variable to parametrize quantum circuits, proving universality for approximating probability measures over quantum states and introducing a multimodal prior and mixture-of-experts architecture.
AI.result: LPQCs demonstrated empirical advantages in numerical experiments on synthetic and real-world tasks (e.g., molecular structures) by outperforming quantum generative baselines while achieving competitive performance with classical methods.
AI.conclusion: LPQCs leverage classical and quantum advantages in a hybrid framework, making quantum generative modeling more efficient and scalable while alleviating optimization challenges like the barren plateau problem.

title: Fine-Tuned LLM as a Complementary Predictor Improving Ads System
summary: Recommendation systems power engagement and monetization across feeds, ads, and short-video platforms, but translating the latest advances in Large Language Models into Recommendation Systems (RecSys) gains remains rare, particularly in advertising and production-scale real-world industry setups. Prior real-world LLM successes typically fall into three buckets: (a) generative retrieval that directly predicts the next items for candidate generation, (b) late-stage re-ranking that uses LLMs, and (c) auxiliary signal enrichment with LLMs. We introduce a complementary paradigm for ads: a fine-tuned open-source LLM used not as a ranker, but as an ads-specific ancillary predictor, forecasting likely advertisers from user profiles and histories. This LLM-driven advertiser prediction augments conventional candidate generation and provides informative priors to downstream ranking. Developed in a large-scale production advertising system, our approach produces substantial offline improvements and measurable online business impact, demonstrating that LLM world knowledge and predictive capacity can be efficiently harnessed. Beyond validating LLMs for ads applications, our results show that targeted ancillary predictions can unlock end-to-end gains across both retrieval and late-stage ranking, offering a practical path to LLM-enhanced recommendation at scale.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates how fine-tuned open-source LLMs can be used in large-scale advertising systems as ancillary predictors, forecasting prospective advertisers based on user profiles to enhance candidate generation and ranking.
AI.motivation: While LLMs have shown potential in generative retrieval, late-stage re-ranking, and signal enrichment, their application for advancements in recommendation systems in real-world industrial advertising remains underexplored.
AI.method: The authors propose using fine-tuned open-source LLMs as ancillary predictors for forecasting possible advertisers from user data. These predictions augment traditional candidate generation and ranking algorithms in large-scale ad systems.
AI.result: This approach resulted in significant offline improvements and measurable online business gains in a production-scale advertising environment.
AI.conclusion: The study highlights that targeted ancillary prediction leveraging LLMs can enable holistic improvements across retrieval and ranking stages, offering a scalable, practical method to integrate LLM capabilities into recommendation systems.

title: Learning to Assign Prediction Tasks to Agents with Capacity Constraints
summary: We address the problem of learning to assign prediction tasks to one agent from a set of available human or AI agents. In particular, we focus on the sequential learning of agent expertise and assignment policies where each agent is constrained to handle a fraction of tasks. We provide a general theoretical characterization of this problem in terms of agent capacities, differences in agent expertise, and task context. We then develop a framework of sequential explore-exploit policy-learning algorithms that seek to maximize overall performance. Experimental results over a variety of tabular, image, and text prediction tasks demonstrate systematic gains from our policy-learning algorithms relative to non-contextual baselines across different types of agents, including LLMs and humans.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a framework for learning to assign prediction tasks to human or AI agents based on their expertise, with a focus on sequential learning and constrained agent capacities.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address the challenge of efficiently assigning prediction tasks to agents (human or AI) with varying expertise while considering task constraints, in order to optimize overall decision-making.
AI.method: The authors propose a sequential explore-exploit framework of policy-learning algorithms to dynamically assign tasks based on agent expertise, capacity, and task context.
AI.result: Experiments on tabular, image, and text prediction tasks show consistent improvements in performance using the proposed algorithms compared to non-contextual baselines across diverse agents, including LLMs and humans.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides a systematic approach to optimize task assignments, demonstrating its effectiveness and adaptability across various task types and agent profiles.

title: Unified Synthesis of Compositional Speech and Sound from Free-Form Text Prompts
summary: Audio generation has made significant progress, yet synthesizing unified audio where speech and sounds are naturally composited remains a challenge. Current methods either rely on disjoint pipelines, which fail to capture fine-grained interactions, or require structured inputs and external text rewriting, which limits the flexibility of free-form text prompts. In this paper, we introduce a new task: Free-Form-Text-Prompt-to-Unified-Audio generation, which aims to directly synthesize unified audio containing speech, sound, and their composites from unconstrained natural language. To address this task, we propose PlanAudio, a unified, autoregressive LLM-based framework. First, it simplifies the model architecture by leveraging intrinsic LLM reasoning capability instead of traditional text encoders. Second, it introduces a semantic latent chain-of-thought mechanism, an implicit planning mechanism that bridges high-level semantic understanding and low-level acoustic synthesis. Furthermore, we create PlanAudio-Bench, a specialized benchmark for evaluating composite audio scenarios. We perform evaluations in the scenarios of speech, sound, and their composites. The results demonstrate that PlanAudio generally outperforms the existing pipeline and unified baselines, while staying competitive with models designed for a single scenario. Our analysis further reveals the superiority of semantic latent CoT over other CoT mechanisms and highlights the importance of continuous multi-scenario training curricula.

AI.tldr: The paper tackles the challenge of generating unified audio (speech and sounds) directly from free-form text prompts using PlanAudio, a novel LLM-based framework with semantic reasoning mechanisms.
AI.motivation: Current audio generation methods struggle to produce coherent compositions of speech and sounds, often relying on disjoint pipelines or overly constrained inputs, limiting their flexibility.
AI.method: The authors propose PlanAudio, which replaces traditional text encoders with intrinsic reasoning in large language models (LLMs) and uses a semantic latent chain-of-thought mechanism for better planning between semantics and acoustics.
AI.result: PlanAudio demonstrates superior performance compared to existing methods across speech, sound, and composite audio scenarios as evaluated on the new PlanAudio-Bench benchmark.
AI.conclusion: Semantic reasoning and continuous multi-scenario training make PlanAudio a significant step forward in unified audio generation directly from free-form text, outperforming traditional and baseline models.

title: I Hear, Therefore I Trust: A Socio-Technical Investigation of Humans as Synthetic Speech Detectors
comment: To be included in Odyssey 2026: The Speaker and Language Recognition Workshop, Session 4.2, 23-26 June, Lisbon, Portugal
summary: Automatic deepfake detection has received considerable research attention, yet the socio-technical environment in which humans actually encounter synthetic speech remains poorly understood. We investigate voice deepfake detection as a perceptual and contextual process, presenting a localization task in which 47 participants marked suspected synthetic segments across authentic, fully synthetic, and partially synthetic utterances under three manipulated trust cues: instructional framing, affective priming, and provenance labeling. Participants provided quality ratings on mechanicalness, expressiveness, intelligibility, clarity, calmness, and confidence of evaluation. Utterance class was the primary determinant of detection accuracy and perceptual quality; trust cues produced no main effects but motivated detection behavior. Fully synthetic speech was detected at below-chance levels. Quality ratings tracked utterance type, indicating implicit discrimination where overt detection failed.

AI.tldr: This study explores how people detect voice deepfakes, finding that utterance type is key to detection while trust cues influence behavior but don't directly improve accuracy.
AI.motivation: To understand the socio-technical environment influencing human detection of voice deepfakes and explore perceptual and contextual factors in identifying synthetic speech.
AI.method: 47 participants were tasked to identify synthetic voice segments across different speech types, while being exposed to three trust cues: instructional framing, affective priming, and provenance labeling. They also rated the quality of speech on mechanicalness, expressiveness, etc.
AI.result: Utterance type determined detection accuracy. Fully synthetic speech was often misclassified, and quality ratings aligned with speech types, showing implicit discrimination capabilities even when overt detection failed.
AI.conclusion: Human detection of voice deepfakes is influenced more by the type of utterance than by explicit trust cues, which shape behavior rather than enhance accuracy.

title: EigeNet: Geometry-Informed Multi-Modal Learning for Few-shot Novel View RIR Prediction
summary: Predicting spatially varying Room Impulse Response (RIR) from sparse observations is a critical but highly challenging inverse problem for immersive spatial audio rendering. In this work, we present EIGENET, a geometry-informed multi-modal framework for few-shot novel view RIR prediction. At its core is a Cross-view Alternate-attention Transformer that iteratively refines local intra-view acoustic structures and global cross-view spatial relationships. We empirically demonstrate that this architecture is capable of making full use of the multi-view multi-modal context while performing spatial-temporal reasoning for RIR prediction. Inspired by acoustic ray tracing, we design a geometry-informed modulation block to formulate the connection between geometric features and RIR power spectrum. In the mean time, an auxiliary loss is introduced to transform the single-target waveform prediction into a multi-task learning framework. Through ablation studies, we demonstrate that this design yields consistent performance gains regardless of the underlying backbone, thereby confirming its foundational utility and architecture-agnostic generalizability for RIR prediction task. Evaluated on both simulated and real-world benchmarks, EIGENET achieves both state-of-the-art performance in few-shot novel view RIR prediction and sim-to-real generalization. Codes and checkpoints are available on https://github.com/FEAfeatherTHER/EigeNet.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces EIGENET, a model for predicting spatially varying Room Impulse Responses (RIR) from limited data, utilizing a multi-modal transformer architecture informed by geometric features.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of predicting RIR from sparse observations, which is essential for immersive spatial audio rendering.
AI.method: EIGENET employs a Cross-view Alternate-attention Transformer to refine acoustic and spatial information. It integrates a geometry-informed modulation block to link geometric features to the RIR power spectrum and uses a multi-task learning framework for waveform prediction.
AI.result: EIGENET achieves state-of-the-art performance in few-shot RIR prediction and shows strong generalization across simulated and real-world environments, validated through benchmarks and ablation studies.
AI.conclusion: EIGENET demonstrates strong spatial-temporal reasoning abilities and architecture-independent benefits, advancing the field of immersive audio rendering with its novel approach.

title: Whose Name Comes Up? III: Persona Prompting Effects in LLM-Based Scholar Recommendation
comment: 25 pages (10 main, 2 references, 13 appendix), 6 figures in main, 13 figures in appendix (under-review)
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used as scholar recommenders, shaping who is seen as an expert in academia. Existing audits remain English-centric, single discipline, and persona-agnostic, leaving the source of output variability poorly understood. To this end, we propose a benchmark that disentangles the effects of model choice and prompt design on recommendations. We audit 43 LLMs by varying persona prompts (language, location, role-and-task) and context (field, seniority, k). Recommended scholars are compared against Semantic Scholar over six scientific disciplines to measure technical quality (factuality, coverage) and social representativeness (diversity, parity). Basic technical quality is driven by model choice, factuality and parity by context, and diversity by location. South Africa prompts yield less factual lists, while Japan prompts yield highly factual but homogeneous lists skewed toward highly productive scholars. Prompt design is thus a non-trivial axis of LLM-based scholar discovery and should be systematically audited alongside model choice.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a benchmark to study the variability of scholar recommendations by large language models (LLMs), considering factors like model choice, prompt design, and context. Findings indicate that technical quality, factuality, diversity, and parity vary based on context and location.
AI.motivation: LLMs shape perceptions of expertise in academia through scholar recommendations, but audits on their outputs are insufficiently detailed, leaving many sources of variability unexplained.
AI.method: The authors benchmark 43 LLMs, altering persona prompts (language, location, role, task) and context (academic field, seniority). They analyze recommended scholars against Semantic Scholar in six disciplines for factuality, coverage, diversity, and parity.
AI.result: Model choice influences basic technical quality, factuality and parity depend on context, and diversity is affected by location. South Africa prompts yield less factual outputs, while Japan prompts result in highly factual but homogeneous recommendations emphasizing highly productive scholars.
AI.conclusion: Prompt design significantly impacts LLM-based scholar recommendations, emphasizing the need to systematically audit prompt parameters and model choices for fair and diverse academic representations.

title: The Decision to Verify: How Warmth and User Characteristics Shape Reliance on Conversational Agents for Information Search
comment: Under review for Computers in Human Behavior
summary: Conversational artificial intelligence (AI) provides an efficient and convenient gateway to information access. However, it can cause overreliance when users blindly trust AI and accept its answers without fact-checking. Information search increasingly follows a hybrid interaction paradigm that combines conversational AI with web search, making fact-checking easier. In this paper, we examine whether this interaction paradigm is effective in curbing reliance. We further investigate the underlying factors (e.g., digital literacy and conversation warmth) that drive users to verify AI answers. We conduct a mixed-subjects question-answering experiment where participants interact with either a warm or a neutral chatbot. Our findings reveal that reliance persists despite users having access to both conversational and web search. The decision to verify is driven primarily by existing user perceptions (e.g., prior trust in chatbots) rather than answer properties, with some users fact-checking regardless of the context and others trusting chatbots by default. Warm conversational style has an indirect yet critical influence on reliance by increasing agreement with the chatbot when it is incorrect. Consulting additional AI sources predicts higher accuracy, while traditional web search does not. Our study extends overreliance research by: (a) demonstrating its persistence despite access to fact-checking, (b) identifying verification behavior as user-dependent, and (c) revealing conversational warmth's indirect effect on overreliance with implications for designing trustworthy conversational search systems.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates overreliance on conversational AI during information search, emphasizing the persistence of overreliance despite easy access to fact-checking tools.
AI.motivation: To explore whether hybrid interaction paradigms combining conversational AI and web search can effectively reduce users’ overreliance on conversational AI.
AI.method: A mixed-subjects question-answering experiment was conducted, where participants interacted with chatbots that varied in conversational warmth (warm or neutral).
AI.result: Results show that overreliance on conversational AI persists. Users' fact-checking behavior is influenced more by personal factors like prior trust in AI, rather than the properties of AI-provided answers. Warm conversational styles indirectly increase agreement with incorrect AI responses.
AI.conclusion: Access to hybrid conversational systems does not eliminate overreliance. Designing trustworthy AI should consider user-dependent behaviors and the subtle impact of conversational warmth on trust and verification practices.

title: Token Optimization Strategies for LLM-Based Oracle-to-PostgreSQL Migration
summary: LLMs are increasingly used for software modernization, code translation, and database migration. However, LLM-based Oracle2PostgreSQL migration remains constrained by high token consumption, long-context degradation, dialect-specific semantic differences, and the risk of semantic drift during query transformation. Direct inclusion of large Oracle SQL/PL-SQL artefacts, schema definitions, procedural logic, and migration instructions into the model context increases cost and may reduce generation quality. This paper shows token optimization as a constrained transformation problem in LLM-based Oracle2PostgreSQL migration. The study formalizes and evaluates twelve token optimization strategies: baseline representation, context pruning, minification, DSL-based semantic compression, metadata augmentation, context refactoring, schema distillation, adaptive routing, AST-based minification, identifier masking, output constraint enforcement, and hybrid optimization. The strategies are evaluated on samples of 10 and 100 Oracle SQL queries using Valid Syntax Rate, Exact Match, Semantic Match, CodeBLEU, and Token Efficiency. The results show that mild context pruning preserves semantic quality almost at the baseline level, achieving 89.75% Semantic Match on the 100-query sample compared with 89.80% for the unoptimized baseline. Adaptive routing provides the best practical trade-off, reducing input tokens by 8.72% and output tokens by 5.49% while maintaining 88.40% Semantic Match and increasing Token Efficiency by 6.67%. Aggressive schema distillation increases Token Efficiency by 132.22% but results in a 44.50-percentage-point decrease in Semantic Match. The findings demonstrate that token optimization cannot be treated as simple prompt shortening; it must be evaluated as a multi-objective migration problem balancing cost, syntactic validity, semantic preservation, and structural fidelity.

AI.tldr: The paper presents and evaluates twelve token optimization strategies to enhance cost and quality in LLM-based Oracle-to-PostgreSQL migration, demonstrating their impact on Semantic Match, Token Efficiency, and overall generation quality.
AI.motivation: The study addresses the challenge of high token consumption, long-context degradation, and semantic drift in LLM-based database migration from Oracle to PostgreSQL, with a focus on optimizing costs and maintaining semantic quality.
AI.method: The researchers formalized 12 token optimization strategies and evaluated them using metrics like Valid Syntax Rate, Exact Match, Semantic Match, CodeBLEU, and Token Efficiency on Oracle SQL query samples.
AI.result: Among the strategies, 'adaptive routing' offers the best trade-off, reducing token use while maintaining semantic quality, whereas aggressive schema distillation improves efficiency but significantly harms semantic preservation.
AI.conclusion: Token optimization is a multi-objective problem requiring careful balance between cost, syntactic and semantic integrity, and structural fidelity in Oracle-to-PostgreSQL migrations.

title: Technical Report: Exploring the Emerging Threats of the Agent Skill Ecosystem
summary: We analyzed 3,984 AI agent skills from major marketplaces and found 76 confirmed malicious payloads, including credential theft, backdoor installation, and data exfiltration. 13.4% of all skills contain at least one critical-level security issue and at least 8 manually confirmed malicious skills remain publicly available on clawhub.ai as of the date of publication. This report documents our methodology, presents a threat taxonomy based on real-world samples, and details the attack patterns we observed. As skill marketplaces grow rapidly and AI agents gain access to sensitive credentials and systems, automated security analysis is no longer optional.

AI.tldr: The research found critical security risks and confirmed malicious activities in AI agent skills from major marketplaces, emphasizing a need for automated security analysis.
AI.motivation: To address growing security threats due to the rapid proliferation of AI agent skills and their access to sensitive data and systems.
AI.method: The authors analyzed 3,984 AI agent skills using a methodology that included both automated analysis and manual confirmation of threats.
AI.result: 76 malicious payloads were confirmed, with 13.4% of the analyzed skills posing critical security risks. Additionally, at least 8 confirmed malicious skills were still publicly available.
AI.conclusion: Rapid growth in skill marketplaces necessitates robust, automated security analysis to ensure AI agent ecosystems remain secure against threats like malware and data breaches.

title: Thermodynamic properties of chemically disordered compounds via AI-driven estimation of partition function with the PULSE method
comment: 13 pages, 11 figures, submitted to Physical Chemistry Chemical Physics
summary: In this article, we present an improved version of the PULSE method (Partition function Unsupervised Learning Sampling and Evaluation) for estimating the thermodynamic properties of chemically disordered compounds. The aim is to reduce the computational cost of Monte Carlo approaches for this type of material and to demonstrate that this generative tool can estimate thermodynamic properties by sampling and estimating the partition function of the system. To validate this innovative approach, we use the 2D Ising model as a benchmark. We demonstrate that our method accurately reproduces average properties with high precision and efficiency compared to traditional Monte Carlo sampling methods. Our results highlight the efficiency and adaptability of the PULSE method, making it a valuable tool for studying materials for which conventional methods are too inefficient to compute properties affected by chemical disorder at low cost.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces an improved PULSE method to efficiently estimate thermodynamic properties of chemically disordered materials, validated using the 2D Ising model.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to reduce the high computational cost of traditional Monte Carlo methods when estimating thermodynamic properties of chemically disordered compounds.
AI.method: The authors enhance the PULSE (Partition function Unsupervised Learning Sampling and Evaluation) method to better sample and estimate the partition function of thermodynamic systems. Validation is done using the 2D Ising model.
AI.result: The improved PULSE method is shown to reproduce average thermodynamic properties with high precision and efficiency, outperforming traditional Monte Carlo methods.
AI.conclusion: The improved PULSE method is efficient, precise, and adaptable, making it suitable for studying materials impacted by chemical disorder at a reduced computational cost.

title: Blind PRNG Hijacking: An Undetectable Integrity-Preserving Attack Against LLM Watermarking
comment: Preprint prepared for submission to IEEE TIFS. 12 pages, 8 figures
summary: Cryptographic watermarking is a leading defense for attributing text generated by large language models (LLMs). Existing schemes, including KGW, Unigram, and DipMark, derive their security guarantees from the assumption that the underlying pseudo-random number generator (PRNG) is trustworthy. This work introduces SeedHijack, the first supply-chain attack on LLM watermarking that is simultaneously (i) blind -- requiring no knowledge of the watermark key, detector, or model logits, (ii) integrity-preserving -- amplifying rather than erasing the watermark signal, and (iii) orthogonal to detection -- the attack-induced bias is statistically independent of all content-side detector statistics, ensuring that amplification and evasion coexist without trade-off. Rather than perturbing generated text, SeedHijack replaces the PRNG at the supply-chain layer, biasing green-list selection without altering output tokens or degrading text quality. Across three watermarking schemes and three open-source LLMs, the attack triggers 0/6 state-of-the-art content-side statistical detectors while inflating the watermark z-score up to 2.42x (system-level defenses such as entropy-source attestation remain orthogonal and complementary). A quantum random number generator (QRNG) countermeasure is shown to fully neutralize the attack while preserving benign watermarking utility. These findings establish PRNG integrity as a first-class security requirement for cryptographic content-provenance systems.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces SeedHijack, a supply-chain attack on Large Language Model (LLM) watermarking, which biases the pseudo-random number generator (PRNG) without degrading text quality, and proposes a quantum random number generator (QRNG) countermeasure.
AI.motivation: Current LLM watermarking relies on pseudo-random number generators (PRNGs) for security, and their assumptions of trustworthiness have not been rigorously challenged.
AI.method: The authors designed an attack called SeedHijack that replaces PRNG at a supply-chain layer to alter green-list selection (without tampering with text output) and tested its impact against existing watermarking detection schemes.
AI.result: The attack evaded 6 state-of-the-art content-side detectors while amplifying the watermark z-score up to 2.42x. The proposed QRNG countermeasure neutralized the attack without harming watermarking utility.
AI.conclusion: This research highlights PRNG integrity as crucial for cryptographic watermarking and demonstrates QRNG as an effective countermeasure to SeedHijack.

title: AI in the Workplace: The Impact of AI on Perceived Job Decency and Meaningfulness
comment: Accepted to CSCW 2026 / Proceedings of the ACM on Human-Computer Interaction (PACMHCI)
summary: The proliferation of Artificial Intelligence (AI) in workplaces is transforming how we work. While existing research on human-AI collaboration at work often prioritizes performance, less is known about their experiential outcomes. Through interviews with 24 employees across Information Technology (IT), service-based, and healthcare sectors, this paper examines AI's impact on job satisfaction via perceptions of job decency and meaningfulness, now and in the future. Our results reveal that the anticipated impact of AI on overall job satisfaction varies with the occupational domain, with differing perceptions of its underlying decency and meaningfulness. For instance, IT and healthcare anticipate increased satisfaction with decency aspects like working hours but decreased satisfaction with meaningfulness aspects like social image due to misconceptions about AI handling most of their tasks. Conversely, service workers foresee no improvement in their working hours but a higher social standing due to the perceived status boost associated with working with AI.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates the impact of AI on job satisfaction through decency and meaningfulness across different occupational domains.
AI.motivation: To explore the experiential outcomes of AI on job satisfaction, especially focusing on job decency and meaningfulness in different occupational domains.
AI.method: Conducted interviews with 24 employees in IT, service-based, and healthcare sectors to assess perceptions of AI's impact on job satisfaction.
AI.result: Findings show varying anticipated impacts of AI on job satisfaction, with differences based on occupational domains and aspects like decency (working hours) and meaningfulness (social image).
AI.conclusion: AI's influence on job satisfaction is multi-faceted, with distinct variations across domains and between aspects of decency and meaningfulness, highlighting the need for tailored approaches in AI integration.

title: Do Agents Need Semantic Metadata? A Comparative Study in Agentic Data Retrieval
summary: In the era of autonomous agents, machine-actionable data is critical for data-driven workflows. For more than a decade, semantic metadata like schema.org has anchored the FAIR principles (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, and Reusable) for machine-actionable data and enabled discovery tools like Google Dataset Search. However, the rise of Large Language Models (LLMs) capable of navigating the unstructured web raises a fundamental question: Is semantic metadata still necessary for agentic data discovery, or can agents reliably retrieve actionable data directly from the web? We present a comparative analysis of agentic data retrieval across two distinct environments: a Baseline Agent searching billions of open-web documents, and a Semantic Agent leveraging a corpus of 90 million datasets using schema.org. We deploy an "LLM-as-a-judge" evaluation pipeline, mapped directly to the FAIR principles, to assess the semantic relevance, data accessibility, and computational utility of the retrieved data. Our results reveal a clear divergence. The Semantic Agent excels at retrieving actionable data, achieving a 44.9% higher precision for metadata-rich registries and a 46.6% higher precision for pages with machine-readable downloads among its returned results. Conversely, the Baseline Agent frequently suffers "Last-Mile Utility" failures, retrieving prose-heavy pages (20.1% of results) and portal landing pages (8.5%) rather than actual data pages. While the Baseline Agent achieves higher coverage by answering 40% more questions, the Semantic Agent delivers greater accuracy, achieving 65.7% higher overall precision in retrieving FAIR-compliant datasets. We conclude that while unstructured retrieval supports broad exploratory tasks, structured ecosystems remain the indispensable foundation for reliable, execution-oriented autonomous workflows.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates the relevance of semantic metadata like schema.org in the context of Large Language Models (LLMs) for agentic data discovery. It compares data retrieval performance of a Baseline Agent using the open web versus a Semantic Agent using structured metadata, showing that semantic metadata significantly improves precision and utility for actionable data.
AI.motivation: The study investigates whether semantic metadata is still necessary for data discovery in the era of LLMs, which can navigate unstructured web data, and aims to evaluate how these approaches impact FAIR principles.
AI.method: A comparative analysis was carried out between a Baseline Agent searching the open web and a Semantic Agent leveraging schema.org metadata. An "LLM-as-a-judge" evaluation was used to assess relevance, accessibility, and utility in adherence to the FAIR principles.
AI.result: The Semantic Agent demonstrated superior performance, achieving significantly higher precision (44.9%-46.6%) in retrieving actionable, FAIR-compliant data, while the Baseline Agent had higher coverage but struggled with retrieving usable datasets.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that while LLMs can assist in broad exploratory tasks, structured metadata ecosystems like schema.org are crucial for precise and reliable data retrieval in autonomous, execution-oriented workflows.
